item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article as missing the

« SCP-2277 | SCP-2278 | SCP-2279 »

I added it in.

item.title: Edit
item.content:encoded: The very last paragraph says “in situ containment” just a quick edit tip

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
This image is borrowed from:

Original Source: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/65/Loreley_in_fog.JPG
Creator: Kim Traynor
License: CC BY-SA 4.0

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You didn't tell me you uploaded this~! Awww, this makes me so very happy! The concept behind this is spectacular, the article is creative and impressive, and the audio makes the atmosphere peak like no other article has done for me. Extremely enthusiastic +1~!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I totally get it, now. I didn't really have a way to put together the pieces of this skip, and then the Genius Loci and what that had implied, until this explanation here. Now that I get it, the experience of reading it has increased exponentially. I love it, now, and I can only hope for more like it. It has the perfect opportunity to become much bigger, in a sense. A wonderful concept to expand upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, my canvas was the memetic evolution of a modern legend. SCP-2278 isn't Loreley. Or rather, not only. It's the collective unconscious gestalt surrounding it, made material. So, it's Loreley, and every mermaid, and the Rhine itself. All of that and none of that.

The whale part is mermaids explained as cetacean "songs", the decay is that of the legend itself, the gold and the red water are the Rhine. All merged together into one anomalous nexus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The legend of Loreley. A mermaid or the like living on or in a rock in the river Rhein. There is a quite famous poem by Heinrich Heine, naming the mermaid and describing her melancholy-inducing song. The area has been a hotspot for ship accidents for centuries: The river currents are treacherous enough, and once when the river had very little water I could see lots of small, sharp rocks just peeping out of the water; as the water is usually quite mirky they are invisible at normal water levels. So there is plenty of normal potential for accidents there. In addition, the area is (rightfully) renowned for being the most beautiful part of the river valley. This combination led to widespread belief (or rather a very popular legend) of that mermaid's song causing these accidents. Except that I am unaware of anything whaly involved in that - the maid is just described as a blond and beautiful mermaid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So then what is this a reference to? Whatever this is, I have never heard of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I am going to listen to it again with that in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It includes manipulated whalesong, but that's only one layer. The main background comes from a soundscape I derived from a Liszt lied.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful audio! Was it actually manipulated whalesong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Few tone issues here and there (the contractions especially), and species names (Balaenoptera musculus, Homo sapiens) should be italicized. But the audio sells it for me, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The audio really gives a great atmosphere to reading the rest of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No real reason, actually. Moved it to the very top.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tiny little quibble, but is there a reason that the image is placed lower than on most articles? Generally, image coding follows immediately after the rating module.
